summary.brief: Harris v. Schirmer. Medical malpractice case involving oncology.

evidence_index.text: On the night of April 6,2011, Mark Harris (“Mark”) was admitted to the emergency department at Carilion Stonewall Jackson Hospital in Lexington, Virginia with various symptoms, including: tremors, fever, diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, restlessness, myalgias, and a headache.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Prior to his admission to the emergency room, Mark had taken 600 mg of Dextromethorphan, an over-the-counter cough medication.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: It is medically established that when one mixes Selegiline with Dextromethorphan, the risk of serotonin syndrome, a potentially fatal condition that affects specific neurotransmitters called serotonin, is likely.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Schirmer, the emergency room physician on April 6,2011, attempted to treat Mark.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mark was indeed suffering from serotonin syndrome as a result of the interaction between the Selegiline in his system and his ingestion of Dextromethorphan.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: He was eventually pronounced dead at 1:31 a.m. on April 7, 2011, at the age of twenty.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: An autopsy was conducted and showed that Mark’s death was a result of serotonin syndrome caused by acute Dextromethorphan and Selegiline toxicity.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: After deliberation, the jury returned a verdict in favor of Defendants.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court is not to determine whether the evidence conclusively proved that Mark Harris was contributorily negligent, and, therefore, barred from recovery.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Because Dr. Schirmer’s failure to diagnose and Mark’s concealment of facts critical to his treatment were sufficiently concurrent with each other, the contemporaneousness requirement was satisfied.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Tremors, fever, diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, restlessness, myalgias, and headache
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient admitted to the emergency department with symptoms suggestive of serotonin syndrome. He had taken 600 mg of Dextromethorphan and had a history of Selegiline use.
past_medical_history: Previous overdose on Selegiline and Vyvanse

focused_exam.general: Patient was alert and oriented, able to follow commands

initial_differential: Other causes of tremors and fever

requestables.name: Toxicology Screen
requestables.description: Screen for presence of drugs and their levels

reveal.clinical_significance: Could confirm presence of Selegiline and Dextromethorphan
reveal.decision_impact: Would support diagnosis of serotonin syndrome

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup
timeline_phases.description: Assessment and initial management in ED

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with symptoms suggestive of serotonin syndrome
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.description: Consider serotonin syndrome and initiate appropriate treatment
options.clinical_reasoning: Symptoms and history suggest serotonin syndrome; treatment should be initiated

options.description: Rule out serotonin syndrome based on patient's denial of recent Selegiline use
options.clinical_reasoning: Patient's denial led to ruling out serotonin syndrome

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant ruled out serotonin syndrome based on patient's denial
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Patient denied recent use of Selegiline

expected_action_court.description: Consider serotonin syndrome and initiate appropriate treatment
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Symptoms and history should have prompted consideration of serotonin syndrome

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to consider serotonin syndrome despite symptoms and history
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Serotonin syndrome should have been considered and treated
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care requires consideration of all possible diagnoses based on symptoms

key_findings: Jury found in favor of Defendants
Contributory negligence due to patient's misleading information

court_findings.finding: No liability due to contributory negligence
No liability due to contributory negligence
court_findings.legal_significance: Patient's misleading information contributed to outcome
